retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Fracture -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients will present with mandibular pain, facial asymmetry, deformity, and dysphagia. Malocclusion, a decreased range of movement of the temporomandibular joint, trismus, or lower lip numbness can also be present. Determining the mechanism of injury is important. In a motor vehicle accident, the patient usually suffers from multiple, compound, or communicated mandibular fractures. Mandibular fracture from an altercation is more likely to be single, simple, and nondisplaced.

retrieved_docs.title: Talar Neck Fractures -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the pathophysiology of talar neck fractures. Evaluate potential complications associated with talar neck fractures, including osteonecrosis, posttraumatic arthritis, and malunion, to inform ongoing patient care. Select appropriate interventions based on the severity of soft tissue damage, bony comminution, and fracture displacement associated with talar neck fractures. Communicate the importance of improving care among the interprofessional team to refine care delivery for patients with talar neck fractures. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Subluxation -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: Patients may also describe specific injury mechanisms, such as neck hyperextension or hyperflexion, which can predispose to vertebral misalignment. Loss of consciousness or altered mental status at the time of the injury may be elicited, which could indicate traumatic brain injury. Some people may have a history of previous neck injuries or underlying conditions predisposing to cervical instability, such as degenerative disk disease, osteoarthritis, or congenital cervical spine anomalies. Other medical information that may impact treatment decisions include allergic history, current medications, and history of immunodeficiency, diabetes mellitus, clotting problems, and neurologic, cardiac, and respiratory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Stress Reaction and Fractures -- Complications -- Joint degeneration and osteoarthritis
retrieved_docs.content: Recurrent stress fractures, particularly in weight-bearing joints, may accelerate cartilage wear and contribute to early-onset osteoarthritis. This is especially concerning in fractures involving the femoral neck or tibial plateau.

retrieved_docs.title: Talus Fracture -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Nonoperative management of nondisplaced talar head fractures and appropriate surgical management of displaced talar head fractures correlate with excellent outcomes. The relatively good prognosis of the talar head as compared to the neck and body is at least partially attributable to its good vascular supply. Regarding talar neck fractures, there is a strong positive correlation between the modified Hawkins-Canale fracture type and the risk of avascular necrosis. [19] [20] Additionally, studies have shown that the degree of fracture displacement, degree of comminution, extent of soft-tissue injury, and success of surgical reduction correlate with the development of osteonecrosis and other outcomes such as post-traumatic osteoarthrosis with rates of osteonecrosis in Type I, II, III, and IV fractures reported at 0 to 15%, 20 to 50%, 100%, and 100%, respectively. [21] [22] The Hawkins sign is a radiographic finding of a thin linear subcortical radiolucency appreciated on post-treatment images which indicate preserved blood flow to the talar body in the setting of talar neck fractures. Talar dome osteochondral defects which are stable and nondisplaced often heal well, while displaced unstable fragments may undergo osteonecrosis if not surgically repaired. [23] [24] Crush comminuted talar body fractures have the worst prognosis of all talar body fractures.

retrieved_docs.title: Talar Neck Fractures -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This activity explores the intricacies of talar neck fractures, emphasizing the critical role of the interprofessional team in the patient's care. Participants gain a comprehensive understanding of the challenges posed by these injuries, such as severe soft tissue damage, bony comminution, and frequent fracture displacement. The consequences of talar neck fractures can be devastating and include permanent stiffness, deformity, and pain, often leading to hindfoot arthrosis. More than 90% of displaced talar neck fractures result in hindfoot arthrosis. The course addresses surgical treatment with open reduction internal fixation as the standard of care for most cases but also addresses nonoperative options for specific injury patterns or cases where surgical intervention is not feasible. Participants learn about the common complications following open fixation, including osteonecrosis, posttraumatic arthritis, and malunion. The course discusses the relationship between high-energy mechanisms and soft tissue injuries as well as the challenges of surgical wound healing.

retrieved_docs.title: Talus Fracture -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Failure to reduce a displaced head or neck fracture can lead to secondary osteoarthrosis or necrosis. [8] As stated above, even with appropriate surgical management, Type III and IV talar neck fractures are associated with up to 100% risk of osteonecrosis. Osteonecrosis and secondary osteoarthrosis are common complications of talar body fractures. Associated fractures of the head or neck increase the likelihood of complications in body fractures. Crush comminuted talar body fractures have a high incidence of a bone loss and nonanatomic reduction, with consequent high rates of avascular necrosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Body Fracture -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications include the delayed union of the fractured mandible. If the fracture of the body of the mandible is bilateral along with the parasymphyseal, or condylar fractures, the airway may get impaired. This impairment is due to the muscular action that pulls the distal mandibular segment backward, resulting in obstruction of the oropharynx by the tongue. There may also be nerve damage such as neuropraxia, in which function takes around 4 to 6 weeks to return, or neurotmesis, in which function may take around 18 months to return.

retrieved_docs.title: Pes Cavus -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Neurologic conditions: hereditary motor and sensory neuropathies (HMSN), cerebral palsy, post-stroke symptoms, anterior horn disease, spinal cord lesions, poliomyelitis, myelomeningocele, polyneuritic syndromes, Parkinson disease, Huntington chorea, Friedreich ataxia, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, leprosy, Roussy-Levy syndrome, Stumpell-Lorrain disease, Pierre-Marie heredotaxy Traumatic: compartment syndrome, talar neck malunion, peroneal nerve injury, knee dislocation, scar tissue, burns, vascular lesions, hindfoot instability, tibial fractures (distal), or calcaneal malunion Post-traumatic bone deformities or ligamentous imbalance or instability often lead to a deformity in this post-traumatic setting. Untreated or undertreated clubfoot Idiopathic or other causes include tarsal coalition, rheumatoid arthritis, ankle osteoarthritis, plantar fibromatosis, varus subtalar joint axis, diabetic foot syndrome. The subtle cavus foot is often categorized within the idiopathic group.

retrieved_docs.title: Talar Neck Fractures -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Extensive reports of high rates of complications following talar neck fractures exist in the literature. [5] [8] Reported rates of osteonecrosis of the talus after talar neck fracture range from 11% to 100%. [11] [16] [17] Clinicians widely consider posttraumatic arthritis to be even more common than necrosis, affecting 30% to 90% of patients, according to reports. [17] [18] [19] [20] Involvement of the subtalar joint accounts for 81% of cases of posttraumatic arthritis. [21] [15] Postoperative infection is a concern given the high incidence of open fracture and soft tissue injury, with a reported deep infection rate of 21%. [15] Nonunion of talar neck fractures is relatively uncommon with rates under 5%, while malunion rates range from 20% to 37%. [15] [22] [23] Varus malunion is the most commonly reported deformity resulting from unappreciated or malreduced medial comminution. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 5 -- Community 2961
retrieved_docs.content: The designation “Allowed qualifier” is used as an etiological label for several serious medical conditions, indicating that it denotes the underlying cause of death (التموت), lower‑extremity ulceration (قرحات الطرفين السفليين), and femoral‑neck fracture (كسر عنق الفخذ). Recognizing “Allowed qualifier” as the causal descriptor for these outcomes helps clinicians classify and communicate the origins of these adverse health events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Osteoporosis/low bone mass (SMQ) includes several fracture‑related conditions and orthopedic interventions. Internal fixation of fractures (تثبيت داخلي للكسر) is a member of the osteoporosis/low bone mass category, reflecting that patients with reduced bone density often require surgical stabilization of broken bones. Hip head replacement (إعاضة رأس الفخذ) also belongs to this category, indicating that severe osteoporotic changes can lead to hip joint damage that necessitates prosthetic replacement. In addition, other fractures are listed as members of osteoporosis/low bone mass, underscoring the broad susceptibility of osteoporotic individuals to multiple fracture types. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians anticipate and manage orthopedic needs in patients with osteoporosis or low bone mass.

Internal‑fixation procedures (identified as “تثبيت داخلي للكسر”) are associated with multiple treatment categories: “fracture or dislocation of hip and femur,” “fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur),” and “fracture or dislocation of radius and ulna.” These associations emphasize that bone quality influences surgical decision‑making for both hip and peripheral limb injuries. Within the osteoporosis SMQ, additional members such as “جراحة مفصل الورك” (hip joint surgery), “إعاضة رأس الفخذ” (hip head prosthesis), and “كسور متعددة” (multiple fractures) illustrate the spectrum of interventions and clinical manifestations that arise in patients with compromised bone health. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 1186
retrieved_docs.content: Closed fracture of intracapsular section of femur is mapped to fracture of neck of femur (hip), indicating that this specific intracapsular injury is a form of hip neck fracture. The category fracture of neck of femur (hip) classifies itself as a hip neck fracture and has هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ) (osteoporosis/low bone density) as a member, reflecting the strong association between reduced bone quality and neck‑of‑femur fractures. Within the هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ) grouping, إعانة رأس الفخذ (hip‑head support) and كسور متعددة (multiple fractures) are listed as members, showing that bone‑density disorders encompass both therapeutic interventions such as hip‑head support and clinical presentations like multiple fractures. Together, these relationships illustrate how a closed intracapsular femur fracture fits into the broader classification of hip neck fractures, how osteoporosis/low bone density underlies these injuries, and how related entities such as hip‑head support procedures and the occurrence of multiple fractures are integrated within the same disease framework, informing comprehensive patient evaluation and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2459
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of anterior fossa and fracture of middle fossa are both classified as إِصابة داخل الجمجمة (intracranial injury). Each represents a specific type of skull‑base fracture, with the anterior fossa fracture involving the front portion of the cranial base and the middle fossa fracture affecting the central region of the cranial base. Both conditions fall under the broader category of injuries inside the skull, highlighting their shared nature as intracranial traumatic lesions.
